title: Toxylon aurantiacum
uses: Toxylon aurantiacum, also known as Strychnine tree, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported benefits. However, the actual medicinal uses are limited due to the toxicity of its main alkaloid, strychnine. The plant has been used in some cases to treat conditions such as rheumatism and snake bites, but its use is not recommended due to the high toxicity and potential for poisoning. In some traditional practices, the plant's seeds have been used to treat conditions like fever and gastrointestinal issues. Nevertheless, the use of Toxylon aurantiacum in modern medicine is generally discouraged due to the risks associated with strychnine toxicity.
taxonomy: Toxylon aurantiacum, also known as the yellow sandalwood or yellow saunders, belongs to the family of Buxaceae and is classified under the genus Toona. The species name Toxylon aurantiacum is sometimes referred to as Toona aurantiaca. More commonly, it is classified under the family Meliaceae, and some sources may still refer to the old classification. The name change in classification indicates the ongoing process in taxonomic research and refinement. This classification may be subject to change as new information and research become available.
benefits: Toxylon aurantiacum, also known as the yellow frankincense tree, is a plant with various health benefits. Its resin has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant is also believed to have antioxidant and immunomodulatory effects, which can help protect against oxidative stress and boost the immune system. Furthermore, the resin has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including pain, digestive issues, and skin problems. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Toxylon aurantiacum for medicinal purposes.
properties: Toxylon aurantiacum, commonly known as the poison tree or poison nut, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential therapeutic properties. Its extracts are believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in managing various health conditions. The plant's seeds contain a toxic compound that may have analgesic and antiseptic properties, making it a subject of interest in natural medicine. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Toxylon aurantiacum for therapeutic purposes. Its use should be avoided due to its toxicity and potential risks to human health.
constituents: Toxylon aurantiacum is known as the Poison Tree, and it is used in traditional medicine.  The active constituents of Toxylon aurantiacum are primarily cardiac glycosides, which have been found to have medicinal properties. These cardiac glycosides are responsible for the plant's pharmacological effects, including its use in treating cardiovascular conditions. The plant's active constituents also include other compounds such as flavonoids and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The specific composition of Toxylon aurantiacum's active constituents can vary depending on factors such as the plant's source and preparation method.
parts: Toxylon aurantiacum is also known as Strychnos nux-vomica, and its medicinal parts include the seeds, leaves, and bark. The seeds are the most commonly used part, as they contain the toxic alkaloids strychnine and brucine. These alkaloids have been used in traditional medicine for various purposes, including as a stimulant and a treatment for certain types of paralysis. However, the seeds are highly toxic and should be handled with caution. The leaves and bark of the plant also contain some toxic compounds, but they are less potent than those found in the seeds.
preparations: Toxylon aurantiacum, also known as Strychnine Tree, has been used in various herbal preparations, primarily for its medicinal and toxic properties. The tree's seeds, bark, and roots are commonly used in traditional medicine. However, due to its high toxicity, its preparations are typically used under the guidance of a qualified medical professional. In some traditional practices, the tree's extracts are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Nonetheless, the use of Toxylon aurantiacum in herbal preparations is often cautioned against due to its potential for severe adverse effects.
side_effects: Toxylon aurantiacum, commonly known as Strychnine Tree, contains toxic compounds that can cause severe side effects if ingested or improperly handled. Ingesting the plant or its seeds can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. In severe cases, it can cause more serious side effects including seizures, tremors, and even death due to muscle paralysis. Long-term exposure to the plant's toxic compounds can also lead to neurological damage and other systemic problems. It is essential to exercise extreme caution when handling the Strychnine Tree, as even small amounts of its toxic compounds can be hazardous.
